name: Tbilisi
summary: Tbilisi is widely regarded as one of the safer capital cities worldwide, with low crime rates and strong police presence. The Georgian capital offers excellent hospitality and cultural experiences, though visitors should remain aware of petty crimes and common tourist scams.
quickVerdict: Tbilisi consistently ranks among the world's safest capitals, with Georgia receiving a Level 1 travel advisory from the US State Department. While violent crime against tourists is extremely rare, visitors should stay vigilant against pickpocketing in crowded areas and avoid scam establishments. The city's excellent public transport, genuine Georgian hospitality, and visible security presence create a welcoming environment for solo travelers, families, and groups alike.

neighborhoods.name: Old Town (Dzveli Tbilisi)
neighborhoods.description: The historic heart of Tbilisi with UNESCO sites and charming cobblestone streets. Generally very safe during day and night, though watch for tourist touts near major attractions like Narikala Fortress and sulfur baths.

neighborhoods.name: Rustaveli Avenue
neighborhoods.description: The main boulevard featuring theaters, museums, and government buildings. Well-lit with regular police patrols and CCTV coverage. Safe for walking at all hours with plenty of cafes and cultural venues.

neighborhoods.name: Vake
neighborhoods.description: Upscale residential district popular with expatriates and locals. Excellent restaurants, bars, and Vake Park. Very safe area with good transport connections and well-maintained streets.

neighborhoods.name: Vera
neighborhoods.description: Trendy neighborhood known for creative spaces, boutique hotels, and hip bars. Generally safe with a lively atmosphere, though exercise standard urban caution late at night.

neighborhoods.name: Saburtalo
neighborhoods.description: Modern residential area with shopping centers and good metro connections. Safe for visitors but less tourist-focused. Can be quieter in evenings away from main roads.

neighborhoods.name: Gldani
neighborhoods.description: Suburban district on the outskirts with fewer tourist amenities. Higher crime rates than central areas and less English spoken. Avoid walking alone at night and stick to main streets if visiting.

scams.name: Bar Bill Scam
scams.description: Attractive locals invite tourists to bars or clubs where they face inflated bills, threats, and pressure to pay excessive amounts (often 200+ GEL). Security guards may intimidate victims.
scams.howToAvoid: Never accept invitations from strangers to bars or clubs. Stick to well-known establishments and always check prices before ordering. If approached aggressively, leave immediately.

scams.name: Taxi Overcharging
scams.description: Unlicensed taxis and some airport drivers quote inflated fares or claim broken meters. Street taxis may charge tourists 3-5 times the normal rate.
scams.howToAvoid: Always use ride-hailing apps like Bolt or Yandex Go. Never use unofficial airport taxis or drivers who approach you. Agree on fares beforehand if using street taxis.

scams.name: Currency Exchange Fraud
scams.description: Unofficial exchange points offer poor rates or provide counterfeit bills. Some operators shortchange tourists or use sleight-of-hand techniques.
scams.howToAvoid: Only exchange money at banks, official exchange offices, or ATMs. Count money carefully before leaving and verify bills. Avoid street exchangers completely.

scams.name: Restaurant Overcharging
scams.description: Some tourist-area restaurants add unlisted items to bills or charge inflated prices for basic items like bread or water. Occasionally includes fake service charges.
scams.howToAvoid: Always review menus and prices before ordering. Ask about any charges not clearly listed. Check bills carefully and request itemized receipts. Research restaurant reviews beforehand.

soloFemale.overview: Tbilisi is considered very safe for solo female travelers, ranking among the world's top cities for women's safety. Georgian culture deeply respects women, and harassment is rare. The city has excellent public transport, visible police presence, and a strong tradition of hospitality toward visitors. Women regularly explore the city independently, even at night, while taking standard urban precautions.
tips: Use ride-hailing apps like Bolt instead of street taxis, especially at night
Dress modestly when visiting religious sites - carry a scarf for church visits
Be cautious of drink spiking in bars - never leave drinks unattended or accept from strangers
Stay in well-lit areas and inform someone of your plans when out late
Trust Georgian hospitality but maintain normal travel vigilance in tourist areas

nightSafety.overview: Tbilisi offers vibrant and relatively safe nightlife, with many venues operating until dawn. The city center, Old Town, and trendy areas like Vera remain well-patrolled and lively throughout the night. However, avoid isolated areas, stick to reputable establishments, and use reliable transport. The famous techno scene centered around Bassiani and other clubs attracts international visitors safely.
tips: Stick to well-known venues like Bassiani, Khidi, or established bars in Vera and Old Town
Avoid the Kura River area late at night and deserted side streets
Use Bolt or Yandex Go for late-night transport - never hail unmarked taxis
Stay with groups when possible and keep emergency contacts handy
Be cautious of bar scams and always verify establishment reputations beforehand

transport.metro: Tbilisi metro is very safe, clean, and efficient with two lines covering major areas. Operates 6 AM to midnight daily. Well-lit stations with security cameras and regular patrols. Use Metromoney card for 1 GEL rides with 90-minute transfers. Generally safe even for solo travelers at night.
transport.rideshare: Bolt and Yandex Go are the main ride-hailing apps, offering safe, affordable transport with upfront pricing and GPS tracking. Highly recommended over street taxis. Both apps work well citywide with good driver availability. Fares significantly lower than Western cities.
transport.taxis: Official white taxis are regulated but avoid unlicensed vehicles. Many drivers don't speak English and may overcharge tourists. Airport taxis particularly problematic with inflated rates. Street taxis should be avoided - use apps instead for transparency and safety.
transport.tips: Public transport is extremely affordable and safe. Always use ride-hailing apps for taxis. Metro operates until midnight with excellent safety record. Bus system comprehensive with same Metromoney card. Walking is safe in central areas but watch for uneven sidewalks.

customs: Georgian hospitality is legendary - if invited to a home, accept graciously and expect abundant food and wine
Toasting is sacred - maintain eye contact, participate in supra (feast) toasts, and never toast with beer (only wine)
Remove shoes when entering homes and dress modestly in churches - women should cover heads and wear long skirts/pants
Respect hierarchy and age - greet elders first, show deference to older people and those in authority positions
Accept food offerings - refusing outright is considered impolite, even if you only take small portions

health.overview: Georgia requires mandatory health insurance for all tourists entering from January 1, 2026, with minimum coverage of 30,000 GEL. Healthcare quality is good in Tbilisi with modern private clinics and English-speaking doctors, though expensive without insurance. Rural areas have more limited facilities.
health.water: Tap water in Tbilisi is generally safe to drink with good municipal infrastructure. However, visitors with sensitive stomachs may prefer bottled water, which is widely available and inexpensive.
health.vaccinations: No specific vaccinations required for Georgia. Standard travel vaccines (hepatitis A/B, typhoid) recommended. Tuberculosis is present but not a major tourist concern with basic precautions.
health.altitude: Tbilisi sits at low elevation with temperate climate. Summer can be hot and humid, winter mild but variable. Air quality is generally good. Watch for venomous snakes in rural/mountain areas March-October.

emergency.usEmbassy: US Embassy: 29 Georgian-American Friendship Avenue, Tbilisi 0131, Tel: +995 32 227-7000. After hours emergencies: +995 32 227-7000

faq.a: Yes, Tbilisi is very safe for tourists and consistently ranks among the world's safest capitals. Georgia has a Level 1 US travel advisory, violent crime is rare, and the hospitality culture creates a welcoming environment. Standard urban precautions are sufficient.

Tbilisi is generally safe at night, especially in central areas like Old Town, Rustaveli Avenue, and Vera. The city has vibrant nightlife with good security presence. Avoid isolated areas, use ride-hailing apps for transport, and stick to reputable venues.

faq.q: Is Tbilisi safe for solo female travelers?
faq.a: Tbilisi is excellent for solo female travelers, ranking #1 in Georgia for women's safety. Georgian culture respects women, harassment is rare, and many women explore independently including at night. Use standard precautions and ride-hailing apps.

faq.q: What areas should I avoid in Tbilisi?
faq.a: Tbilisi has no strict 'no-go' areas, but exercise more caution in suburban Gldani at night and avoid the Kura River area after dark. Stay alert for tourist touts near major attractions in Old Town during peak season.

faq.q: Is it safe to use public transport in Tbilisi?
faq.a: Yes, public transport is very safe with modern buses, clean metro system, and good security. The metro operates until midnight with cameras and patrols. Use Metromoney card for 1 GEL rides. Transport is more reliable and safer than most European cities.
